blocks:
Ayahs

لِإِيلَـٰفِ قُرَيْشٍ (١)
[In gratitude] for solidarity among the Quraysh, (1)
إِۦلَـٰفِهِمْ رِحْلَةَ ٱلشِّتَآءِ وَٱلصَّيْفِ (٢)
their solidarity during winter and summer journeys, (2)
فَلْيَعْبُدُوا۟ رَبَّ هَـٰذَا ٱلْبَيْتِ (٣)
let them worship the Lord of this House, (3)
ٱلَّذِىٓ أَطْعَمَهُم مِّن جُوعٍ وَءَامَنَهُم مِّنْ خَوْفٍۭ (٤)
who has fed them [and saved them] from hunger, and secured them from fear. (4)

Content and Virtue of Reciting Surah "Quraysh"

This Surah is, in fact, considered a complement to Surah "Al-Fil," and its verses provide clear evidence for this matter.

The content of this Surah describes Allah's blessings upon the Quraysh and His favors and kindness toward them, so that their sense of gratitude might be stimulated, leading them to rise for the worship of the Lord of this Great House (the Kaaba), from which all their honor and pride originate.

As we mentioned at the beginning of Surah "Ad-Duha," that Surah and Surah "Al-Inshirah" are effectively considered one Surah; likewise, Surah "Al-Fil" and Surah "Quraysh" are so closely linked in their subject matter that it serves as evidence for their unity.

For this reason, if one chooses these Surahs to recite as a complete Surah in each unit (Rak'ah) of prayer, they must recite both together.

For further explanation in this regard, one should refer to books of jurisprudence (the Book of Salah, discussion on recitation) [1]. Regarding the virtue of reciting this Surah, it suffices to mention a Hadith narrated from the Noble Prophet صلى الله عليه وآله who said:

"Whoever recites it, he will be given the reward of ten good deeds for every person who has performed Tawaf around the Kaaba or stayed there in I'tikaf." [1]

Certainly, such a virtue belongs to those who bow their heads in submission before Allah, who is the Lord of the Kaaba, worship Him, safeguard the sanctity of this House, and listen to its message with the ear of the soul and put it into practice.

[^1]: The late "Shaykh Hurr al-Amili" in the book "Wasa'il" has brought the narrations related to this matter in Volume 4, page 743 (Chapter 10 of the chapters on prayer recitation). [^1]: "Majma' al-Bayan," Volume 10, page 543.

بِسْمِ اللَّهِ الرَّحْمنِ الرَّحِيمِ‌

لِإِيلافِ قُرَيْشٍ (1) إِيلافِهِمْ رِحْلَةَ الشِّتاءِ وَ الصَّيْفِ (2) فَلْيَعْبُدُوا رَبَّ هذَا الْبَيْتِ (3) الَّذِي أَطْعَمَهُمْ مِنْ جُوعٍ وَ آمَنَهُمْ مِنْ خَوْفٍ (4)

Commentary

The Lord of This House Must Be Worshipped

Since the previous Surah (Surah Al-Fil) described the destruction of the People of the Elephant and the army of "Abrahah," who came with the intention of destroying the Kaaba and dismantling this sacred Divine center, the first verse of this Surah—which is actually a "supplement" to Surah Al-Fil—states: We destroyed the army of the elephant and crushed them like chewed-up straw "so that the Quraysh might become accustomed to this sacred land" and the prerequisites for the appearance of the Prophet of Islam صلى الله عليه وآله would be prepared (لِإِيلافِ قُرَيْشٍ) [1]. "Ilaf" is a verbal noun meaning to cause familiarity or attachment, and "Ulfat" means a gathering combined with harmony, intimacy, and healing. The fact that some have interpreted "Ilaf" as "alliance, covenant, or treaty" is neither consistent with this word (which is the verbal noun of the Bab If'al) nor with the content of the verses of this Surah.

In any case, the purpose is to create an attachment between the Quraysh and the sacred land of Mecca and the House of the Kaaba, because they and all the people of Mecca resided there due to the centrality and security of this land. Many people of the Hijaz came there every year, performed the Hajj rituals, had economic and literary exchanges, and benefited from the various blessings of this land.

All of this was under the shadow of its special security. If this security had been compromised by the invasion of Abrahah and his like, or if the Kaaba had been destroyed, no one would have felt an attachment to this land anymore.

The word "Quraysh," as many commentators and linguists have stated, originally refers to a type of large sea creature that easily eats every other animal! This statement is famous from "Ibn Abbas" that when he was asked: Why are the Quraysh called "Quraysh"? He replied:

"Because of a creature in the sea, among its greatest creatures, called 'al-Quraysh'; it passes by nothing, lean or fat, but that it devours it!" He then cited Arabic poetry as evidence for his words.

Therefore, the choice of this name for the aforementioned tribe was due to their power and strength, and their misuse of this power.

However, some have considered it derived from the root "Qarsh" (rhyming with Farsh) meaning "acquisition," because this tribe was mostly engaged in trade and business.

Some also consider this root to mean "inspection and investigation," and since the Quraysh would inquire about the condition of the pilgrims and sometimes rush to their aid, this word was chosen for them.

"Qarsh" in the language also means "gathering," and because this tribe enjoyed a specific unity and cohesion, this name was chosen for them.

In any case, the name Quraysh today does not evoke a pleasant concept; although they were the tribe of the Noble Prophet صلى الله عليه وآله, they were considered among the most stubborn enemies of Islam, who spared no obstruction, enmity, or hostility. Even on the day their power was broken by the victory of Islam, they continued their secret conspiracies, and after the passing of the Noble Prophet صلى الله عليه وآله, they created painful events that the history of Islam will never forget. We know that the "Umayyads" and the "Abbasids," who were the most prominent examples of tyrannical and Taghuti governments, arose from the Quraysh.

Evidence also shows that during the Pre-Islamic Ignorance (Jahiliyyah), they strove to exploit and colonize people, and for this reason, when the liberating Islam dawned and their illegitimate interests were endangered, they rose to fight with all their might. However, the great power of Islam eventually crushed them.

In the next verse, He adds: "The goal was that Allah would make the Quraysh accustomed to the winter and summer journeys" (إِيلافِهِمْ رِحْلَةَ الشِّتاءِ وَ الصَّيْفِ) [1] [2].

[^1]: The "Lam" in "Li-ilaf" denotes cause, and the "preposition and its object" relate to "Ja'alahum" (He made them) in the previous Surah in the verse "So He made them like chewed-up straw," or another verb from that Surah. Some also consider this related to the phrase "Fal-ya'budu" (So let them worship) which appears in the following verses, but this possibility is not very consistent with the concept of the verses, and the first meaning is better. [^1]: "Ilafihim" is a substitute (Badal) for "Ilaf" in the previous verse. [^2]: "Rihlah" originally comes from "Rahl" meaning the covering placed for riding; then, by association, it was applied to the camel itself or the journeys undertaken with it or other means.

It is possible that the meaning is to make the Quraysh attached to this sacred land so that during their summer and winter journeys, they would not lose their love and affection for this sacred center, and would return to it for its security, lest they be influenced by the advantages of life in the lands of Yemen and Syria and leave "Mecca" empty.

Or it could mean creating an attachment between the Quraysh and other people during these two great journeys, because after the story of Abrahah, people looked at them with different eyes and accorded the Quraysh caravan respect, importance, and security.

The Quraysh needed this security both along the way and in the land of Mecca, and Allah granted them both types of security under the shadow of the defeat of Abrahah's army.

We know that the land of "Mecca" had no gardens or agriculture; its livestock farming was also limited. Most of the income was provided through these commercial caravans. In the winter season, they would head south toward the land of Yemen, where the weather was relatively warm, and in the summer season, toward the north and the land of Syria, which had a mild and pleasant climate. Incidentally, both Yemen and Syria were major centers of trade in those days, and Mecca and Medina were considered the connecting link between the two.

Of course, the Quraysh, with the wrong deeds they committed, did not deserve all this Divine grace and kindness, but because it was predestined that Islam and the Noble Prophet صلى الله عليه وآله would emerge from this tribe and that sacred land, Allah performed this favor for them.

In the next verse, He concludes that the Quraysh, with all these Divine blessings they have found by the blessing of the Kaaba, "should worship the Lord of this House," not the idols (فَلْيَعْبُدُوا رَبَّ هذَا الْبَيْتِ).

"The same God who saved them from hunger and fed them, and delivered them from insecurity and gave them safety" (الَّذِي أَطْعَمَهُمْ مِنْ جُوعٍ وَ آمَنَهُمْ مِنْ خَوْفٍ) [1]. On one hand, He granted them prosperity in trade and brought them benefit, and on the other hand, He removed insecurity from them and repelled harm. All of this was provided through the defeat of "Abrahah's" army and was, in fact, the fulfillment of the prayer of Ibrahim, the founder of the Kaaba. However, they did not appreciate the value of all these blessings and turned this sacred House into a house of idols, prioritizing the worship of idols over the worship of the God of the House, and eventually, they saw the bitter fruit of all this ingratitude.

O Allah! Grant us the success of worship, servitude, gratitude for blessings, and the safeguarding of this Great House. O Lord! Make this great Islamic center more glorious day by day and the connecting link for the Muslims of the world. O God! Cut off the hands of all bloodthirsty enemies and those who misuse this great center! Amen, O Lord of the Worlds.

End of Surah Quraysh.

[^1]: Some commentators have considered this verse as two verses and counted the total verses of this Surah as five, but the famous and well-known view is that it is one verse and the total verses of the Surah are four.
